title: [Closed] VirtualBox provider
body:
Add VirtualBox as a provider for `podman machine`

Add optional argument `--type` to specify provider type for all `podman machine` subcommands (start, stop, ls, ssh, rm, set). Keep old behaviour when `--type` is not provided.

At now, VirtualBox provider has limited support for some reasons such as:
- Fedora CoreOS does not provide images for VirtualBox, and it needs to use user-defined/custom image
- It was checked only on Fedora 35 and macOS 12.1 (Intel), no any sort of warranty for others

**Motivation for this PR**:  Allow using VBox when other options (like QEMU) are not available.
For instance, sometimes there are cases when QEMU couldn't be used or not allowed for usage, it could be for some a company restriction and/or absence of security team approval.

Progress (what was done, what will be):
- [x] MVP version of VirtualBox provider
- [x] podman.socket
- [ ] port forwarding
- [ ] volume mounting
- [ ] Updated `podman machine` cli
- [ ] Documented

[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

Signed-off-by: esendjer <esendjer@gmail.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *esendjer* To complete the [pull request process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process), please assign **giuseppe** after the PR has been reviewed. You can assign the PR to them by writing `/assign @giuseppe` in a comment when ready. The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

> Thanks for opening the PR!
> 
> Can you elaborate on _why_ Podman needs Virtual Box (over the current QEMU implementation)?

Thank you for the question! Sure.
Sometimes there are cases when QEMU couldn't be used or not allowed for usage. For instance, it could be for some a company restriction and/or absence of security team approval (my case, I'm still waiting for this, but we are allowed to use VBox and VMware Fusion for local development), some people are more familiar with VirtualBox but not with QEMU. In the end, diversity and the ability to choose are always better.

> > Thanks for opening the PR!
> > Can you elaborate on _why_ Podman needs Virtual Box (over the current QEMU implementation)?
> 
> Thank you for the question! Sure. Sometimes there are cases when QEMU couldn't be used or not allowed for usage. For instance, it could be for some a company restriction and/or absence of security team approval (my case, I'm still waiting for this, but we are allowed to use VBox and VMware Fusion for local development), some people are more familiar with VirtualBox but not with QEMU. In the end, diversity and the ability to choose are always better.

@vrothberg I suppose I have to clarify my answer a bit. I'm contracting for a financial company and use their equipment/software. QEMU is locked and Docker license is not available for contractors. But they allow VBox for some reasons.

> Not a full review but the type flag should only be added for machine init. We already have the machine so the other commands should get the type from the machine config.

Thanks. Will fix this soon

> Also how does port forwarding work?

I'm not sure that understood this question well, sorry if it is so. The current realisation of the VBox provider creates a NAT network with default configs (default for VBox). Then there adds the rule for forwarding port that was randomly chosen to 22 port in VM, for support SSH connection. This part is fully cared with VBox. In the code it looks so:
```go
// Execute VBoxManager modifyvm with the next options
	"--nic1", "nat",  // Creates a NIC
	"--natpf1", fmt.Sprintf("SSH,tcp,127.0.0.1,%d,10.0.2.15,22", vbm.Port)})  // Creates the rule for forwarding SSH
```

[In the PR](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/13262/files#diff-6a3e3f058cd48b8162cb5bce3f14e3b7152dffce233540479bc4d77f2557dcdfR96-R106)

> Does it support volume mounts?

Unfortunately, doesn't yet. I have ideas how it might be implemented, but currently, it unlikes to a good and simple way. But I'll continue to work on it.

> [...]
> @vrothberg I suppose I have to clarify my answer a bit. I'm contracting for a financial company and use their equipment/software. QEMU is locked and Docker license is not available for contractors. But they allow VBox for some reasons.

That does not sound like a compelling argument to me, unfortunately. Having (yet) another driver for Podman machine adds redundancy and increases maintenance load, also long term. For instance, adding a new feature for provider A likely means doing the same for provider B etc.

I would like other maintainers to speak up and share their thoughts. @containers/podman-maintainers PTAL

I mean port forwarding for `podman run -p ...`. With gvproxy we can forward ports from the host into the vm dynamically. I don't think this works with VitualBox. Also note that this is deeply wired into the libpod code itself. So when you start a container with ports in a machine (`machine_enabled=true` in containers.conf) then it will try to connect to the gvproxy http api. If this fails podman will error. The same for the `host.containers.internal` dns entry, where we use the gvproxy dns server.

I agree with @vrothberg that the added complexity does not seem worth it in the long run, especially if we have to start changing things outside of `cmd/podman/machine/` and `pkg/machine` to make things like port forwarding work.

People can always create their own custom VMs and and connections with `podman system connection add`.

@Luap99 Ah, I see. Thank you for clarifying this point.
For now, I have a question. If the port forwarding will work for VBox provider without any changes in out of `machine` and other mentioned issues will be solved, has this PR  the right to life or has to be closed/removed?
@esendjer I don't have a strong opinion, lets wait for other maintainers. @baude @rhatdan @mheon

I would view this as a contributor problem.  If contributors want to maintain alternative providers, I don't think we should block, but we need to make sure that they provide full support including sockets and volume support. (As well as ignition I would guess.)

> I would view this as a contributor problem. If contributors want to maintain alternative providers, I don't think we should block, but we need to make sure that they provide full support including sockets and volume support. (As well as ignition I would guess.)

I don't see this working out. Some folks will start using it and probably depend on it. If things are merged, we are practically responsible for it. Podman machine is not yet exercised in CI; I don't think we have the cycles to maintain a redundant stack.

I think it is similar to the problem we have with unsupported storage drivers.

Could we have a build tag and not enable it for CentOS/RHEL?
That might be easier to consume. Although I don't know why this is required for Podman machine to do, you could just down load an OS with Podman service running and configure it yourself.

> That might be easier to consume. Although I don't know why this is required for Podman machine to do, you could just down load an OS with Podman service running and configure it yourself.

I concur.  You don't need `podman machine` to talk to a Linux VM running inside VirtualBox. Once, the VM is setup you can use `podman system connection add ...` and podman will start talking to the VM.
